critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-designed. Temporal splitting, single test-set evaluation, preprocessing fitted on training only, and the explicit exclusion of future information are all correct choices. Most of the obvious failure modes have been anticipated. I have two genuine concerns and one lesser issue.

**Issue 1: The 24-hour contact-intensity score is a leakage vector**

The claim is that this feature is 'information available at prediction time.' That may be true in a narrow sense, but the score is a rolling 24-hour aggregate of 'support touches and routing events associated with the customer.' If this score is computed from the production platform's live tables at serving time, its value at training time needs to be reconstructed from the same historical state that existed when each ticket was opened—not from the current-at-training-time state. If the platform's ETL or the feature extraction pipeline uses the full 24-hour window *forward* from ticket creation (i.e., counts interactions that occur *after* the ticket opens), then urgent tickets—which by definition escalate within 2 hours—will naturally accumulate higher contact-intensity values, and the feature leaks the label. The description says the score 'summarizes support touches and routing events,' but routing events triggered *by* the escalation process would be included if the window is not strictly backward-looking. The mechanism: urgent tickets generate more downstream events in the platform, those events fall within a 24-hour window that includes post-open time, and the feature absorbs label signal. This would not be caught by the temporal split, only by auditing the exact window definition and which event types are included.

Evidence: compare model performance with and without the contact-intensity feature. A feature that leaks label information will show a disproportionately large drop in F1 when removed. More definitively, compute the Pearson correlation between the contact-intensity score and the label within the test set conditional on all other structured features—if partial correlation remains high, leakage is the most parsimonious explanation.

**Issue 2: The inter-rater filtering procedure conflates label noise with genuine ambiguity in the hardest cases**

The claim is that excluding tickets with <80% inter-rater agreement removes adversarially noisy examples that corrupt training. But urgency in airline support is genuinely ambiguous precisely in the middle-difficulty cases—the cases where the model needs to work hardest. Agent disagreement at <80% may not indicate label noise; it may indicate that the underlying construct (urgency) is not deterministic. Systematically excluding these cases means the model is trained and evaluated on a distribution that is easier than the deployment distribution, because the ambiguous-but-real cases that reach production are never seen during training or test. The evaluation on the filtered test set will be optimistic, and the model will be underfit to exactly the cases that matter most operationally.

A secondary consequence: the 500-ticket re-labeling sample is used to estimate inter-rater agreement, but it is not clear whether this sample drives filtering of the full 500k dataset or only validates the filtering threshold. If only 500 tickets were re-labeled, the exclusion rule presumably operates on original agent labels alone for the bulk of the data, which is a different, weaker criterion. The methodology should clarify whether inter-rater agreement is estimated at the ticket level (requiring re-labeling of every ticket) or at the category/stratum level (using the 500-ticket sample to derive a reliability estimate and then deciding whether to filter categorically).

Evidence: compare model F1 on the filtered test set versus F1 on the excluded (ambiguous) test tickets. If performance on excluded tickets is substantially worse, the evaluation is inflated. A calibration check (ECE is included, which is good) on the ambiguous stratum specifically would reveal whether the model is well-calibrated where it matters most.

**Minor issue: Baseline is too weak to isolate the mechanism**

The logistic regression baseline uses the same DistilBERT embedding pipeline, but the baseline does not include *structured* features—only the current message CLS token and temporal features. The proposed model adds both structured features *and* historical text jointly. If the proposed model wins, the attribution is ambiguous: the gain could come from structured features alone (customer tier, escalation history, disruption flags), from historical text alone, or from the joint encoding. The methodology claims 'any improvement is attributable to joint encoding,' but that is incorrect unless 'joint encoding' is defined to include all history-related features. A cleaner ablation would include: (a) current message only, (b) current message + structured features, (c) current message + historical text, (d) full model. Without (b) and (c), the value of the transformer architecture for history integration specifically cannot be separated from the value of adding structured history at all. This is a methodological gap but not fatal—it would leave the mechanism claim unsupported without undermining the overall performance claim.
all_issues_raised: The 24-hour contact-intensity score may include post-ticket-open events (escalation-triggered routing), leaking label information into the feature.
Inter-rater filtering excludes genuinely ambiguous cases that exist at deployment, making the evaluation optimistic on the cases the model must handle in production.
The single baseline conflates the effect of structured history features with the effect of historical text encoding, leaving the mechanism attribution unsupported.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 24-hour contact-intensity score may include post-ticket-open events (escalation-triggered routing), leaking label information into the feature.
Inter-rater filtering excludes genuinely ambiguous cases that exist at deployment, making the evaluation optimistic on the cases the model must handle in production.
The single baseline conflates the effect of structured history features with the effect of historical text encoding, leaving the mechanism attribution unsupported.
